questions.q: What is the configuration space (C-space) in robot path planning?
options: The physical workspace where the robot operates
The space of all possible joint angle combinations representing robot configurations
The set of obstacle locations in the environment
The velocity constraints of the robot
questions.explanation: Configuration space represents all possible joint angle combinations (θ₁, θ₂, ..., θₙ). A collision-free path in C-space guarantees no self-collision or collision with obstacles in the physical workspace. The workspace is the physical 3D space; obstacles are represented as forbidden regions in C-space.

questions.q: How does the A* (A-star) algorithm find an optimal path?
options: It randomly samples configurations until a path is found
It uses heuristic guidance (estimated distance to goal) combined with actual travel cost to explore nodes efficiently
It checks every possible path exhaustively
It relies on obstacle positions only
questions.explanation: A* maintains f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where g is actual cost from start and h is heuristic estimate to goal. This balances exploration with goal-directed search, guaranteeing optimality if h is admissible (never overestimates). Random sampling and exhaustive search are different algorithms; heuristics guide but don't ignore costs.

questions.q: What is an advantage of RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree) for path planning?
options: It guarantees finding the optimal path
It works in high-dimensional spaces and can handle complex obstacles without explicit C-space discretization
It requires a discretized grid of the environment
It is faster than A* on all problems
questions.explanation: RRT incrementally builds a tree by randomly sampling C-space and connecting to nearest tree nodes, efficiently exploring high-D spaces without gridding. It finds feasible paths quickly, though not necessarily optimal. A* requires discretization and explicit heuristics; RRT probabilistically complete, not necessarily optimal.

questions.q: What is a potential field method for obstacle avoidance?
options: It builds a grid map of free space
It treats the goal as an attractive force and obstacles as repulsive forces, guiding the robot by gradient descent
It precomputes all safe trajectories offline
It uses a camera to detect obstacles
questions.explanation: Potential field methods model attraction toward the goal and repulsion from obstacles, creating a synthetic force field. The robot follows the steepest descent to the goal. Local minima (getting stuck) can occur near obstacles. Gridding, precomputation, and vision are different techniques.

questions.q: Why might a robot following a potential field get stuck in a local minimum?
options: The goal force becomes too strong
The repulsive forces from obstacles can balance the attractive goal force at locations other than the goal, trapping the robot
Potential fields are inherently unstable
The robot's actuators are too weak
questions.explanation: In concave obstacle configurations, repulsive forces can cancel the goal attraction, creating an equilibrium point (local minimum) before reaching the goal. The robot cannot escape without active control or field modification. Field strength and actuator power are separate; stability depends on field design.

questions.q: What is the difference between a roadmap and a tree in path planning?
options: Roadmaps are only for 2D; trees are for 3D
A roadmap is a graph of feasible paths connecting sampled configurations; a tree is an acyclic graph with a single root, often used by RRT
They are equivalent and interchangeable terms
Roadmaps are computed offline; trees are computed online only
questions.explanation: Roadmaps (like PRM) build graphs with multiple connections between samples, enabling multiple paths. Trees (like RRT) have a root and edges only to nearest neighbors, forming a spanning tree. Both can be online or offline; dimensionality doesn't define the distinction.

questions.q: In motion planning, what does it mean for a path to be collision-free?
options: The path avoids dynamic moving obstacles
The robot and all its links do not intersect obstacles at any point along the path
The path is the shortest possible route
The path follows straight lines only
questions.explanation: Collision-free means no part of the robot (including links and the body) touches obstacles at any configuration along the trajectory. This is checked using collision detection algorithms. Dynamic obstacles require replanning; shortest paths are optimality goals; and straight lines are movement types.

questions.q: What is the workspace path vs. configuration space path in planning?
options: They are the same thing
A workspace path is the physical trajectory in 3D space; a configuration space path is the trajectory in joint angles, which projects to the workspace path
Workspace paths are computed first, then configuration space paths
Configuration space paths do not correspond to physical motion
questions.explanation: A path in C-space (joint angles) defines a trajectory in the physical workspace through forward kinematics. The workspace path is the end-effector's 3D trajectory. Both represent the same robot motion viewed in different coordinate systems. C-space paths always correspond to physical motion in valid workspaces.

questions.q: Why is path smoothing often applied after planning?
options: To reduce computation time during planning
To shorten the path and make it smoother by removing unnecessary waypoints and local irregularities
To verify the path is collision-free
To increase the path length for safety
questions.explanation: Path smoothing (e.g., shortcutting, spline fitting) optimizes a feasible path by eliminating detours and sharp turns, producing shorter, more natural trajectories. It does not improve planning speed; collision-free verification is done during planning; lengthening paths reduces safety.

questions.q: What is a probabilistically complete path planning algorithm?
options: It always finds a path if one exists
It finds a path with high probability if one exists, given sufficient time and samples
It is only complete for 2D environments
It requires an exact map of the environment
questions.explanation: Probabilistically complete algorithms like RRT are guaranteed to find a solution as the number of iterations approaches infinity, but may fail with finite samples. This differs from deterministic completeness (always succeeds). Completeness is independent of dimensionality; exact maps improve but are not required.
